concerns: Some operational specifics are under-specified (e.g., how SES-aware proxies would be defined and legally defended; exact mechanics of capacity caps and thresholds).
Several evidentiary entries rely on non-audited or single-source material (noted in the appendix) and a few links are placeholder/example domains rather than primary datasets.
No explicit cost estimates, implementation timelines, or measurable monitoring/evaluation metrics are provided for the proposed transformations.
Stakeholder mapping (e.g., likely actions by church/private maintainers, teacher unions, parental groups) is discussed qualitatively but lacks a structured actor-risk table.
The package flags contested baseline data (implementation_planning-09) but does not include a recommended measurement strategy to establish a shared evidence baseline prior to reform.
strengths: Well-structured artifact-first package: clear set of mutually comparable transformation options (TP-live-t1…t5) with explicit statutory/political consequences.
Extensive, typified evidence appendix mapping inline finding IDs to sources (URIs/URNs) that supports traceability and auditability for most claims.
Distinct disciplinary lenses (legal, demographic, political-feasibility, implementation, international-comparative, equity) are applied and their limits are acknowledged.
Trade-offs and normative dilemmas are explicitly stated (e.g., structural depth vs deliverability; free parental choice vs constraining segregation).
Practical implementation constraints are surfaced (parliamentary amendment requirements, teacher shortfalls, jurisdictional limits vis-à-vis church/private schools).
Actionable research questions and failure-mode examples are provided to help decision-makers prioritize further evidence-gathering.
